county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Some early territorial records sparse.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Walla Walla County in 1859. Named after the Klickitat tribe. Located along the north side of the Columbia River Gorge. Goldendale is the county seat. The Dalles Dam (1957) inundated Celilo Falls, a traditional Native American fishing site.
county_metadata.research_tips: Klickitat County straddles the Columbia River Gorge and its genealogical landscape reflects both the Native American fishing traditions at Celilo Falls and Euro-American settlement along wagon roads. The Yakama Nation has traditional ties to this area, and Yakama Agency records at NARA Seattle cover families here. Before the Dalles Dam flooded Celilo Falls in 1957, the falls were a major intertribal fishing and trading site — Bureau of Reclamation relocation records document affected families. The county's ranching history is documented in the Klickitat County Historical Society collections in Goldendale. The Central Regional Branch of the State Archives in Ellensburg holds Klickitat County records. Early settlers came via The Dalles on the Oregon Trail — check Oregon side records at Wasco County. FamilySearch has microfilmed early vital records. Goldendale Observatory, while not genealogical, draws visitors who can combine trips with courthouse research.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor recording records from territorial era

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial era. Includes Columbia Gorge properties.
BIA records covering Klickitat band families, Celilo Falls fishing site records, and allotment documents.
Marriage records from territorial era.
Federal census from 1860. Shows ranching and farming families along the Columbia.
Federal land patents in the Columbia Gorge region.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original Oregon Territory county (oldest in WA)
county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire. Some early provisional government records may be at Oregon State Archives.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created in 1845 under the Oregon Provisional Government, making it the oldest existing county in Washington state. Named after Meriwether Lewis of the Lewis and Clark Expedition. Originally covered all of present-day Washington. The county seat moved from various locations to Chehalis. Centralia and Chehalis are the twin cities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lewis County is the oldest existing county in Washington state, created in 1845 before Oregon Territory was even formally established. Its earliest records may be at the Oregon State Archives from the provisional government period. The Centralia Massacre of 1919 (labor conflict between the IWW and American Legion) is documented in the Lewis County Historical Museum and UW Special Collections. George Washington founded Centralia in 1875 — he was a Black pioneer, and the town's multiracial founding is well documented. The county's I-5 corridor location makes it accessible for research visits. The Lewis County Historical Museum in Chehalis has extensive pioneer family files. Fort Borst in Centralia has Civil War-era connections. FamilySearch has microfilmed Lewis County records from the 1850s. The county was parent to many other Washington counties, so early records here may cover a wide geographic area.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from earliest territorial period

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Among the oldest deed records in Washington. Originally covered all of present-day Washington north of the Columbia.
DLCs from earliest settlement. Names wives. Some of the first American land claims in Washington.
Marriage records from earliest territorial period. Among the oldest in Washington.
Federal census from 1850 (Oregon Territory). One of few WA counties in the 1850 census.

source_profiles.source_name: Lewis County Superior Court — Probate Records
source_profiles.repository: WA State Archives / Lewis County Superior Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate files from territorial era including early pioneer estates.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — among oldest in the state.

source_profiles.repository: Chronicling America / Lewis County Historical Museum
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Local newspapers including coverage of the 1919 Centralia Massacre.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Spokane County in 1883. Named after President Abraham Lincoln. Located in the channeled scablands of eastern Washington. Davenport is the county seat. Dryland wheat farming dominates the economy.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lincoln County's channeled scablands landscape attracted dryland wheat farmers, many of them German-Russian immigrants who arrived via the Northern Pacific Railroad in the 1880s-1890s. The Eastern Regional Branch of the State Archives in Cheney holds Lincoln County records. Before 1883, check records under Spokane County. Many Lincoln County families also had connections to neighboring Adams and Grant counties as they homesteaded across the region. German-Russian heritage is well documented in collections at Washington State University Libraries in Pullman. The Lincoln County Historical Society in Davenport has pioneer photographs and family files. The county's German-language newspapers from the late 19th century are on microfilm at the Washington State Library. FamilySearch has microfilmed early vital records from the county. Railroad town plat maps at the Northern Pacific Railway records can identify early lot purchasers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation including railroad land purchases.

source_profiles.repository: WA State Archives Eastern Branch, Cheney
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations and petitions filed by German-Russian and Scandinavian immigrants.
Marriage records from county formation.
Federal census from 1890 (destroyed). German-Russian settlers prominent.
Federal homestead patents in the channeled scablands wheat region.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Some early Sawamish County records may be incomplete.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Originally created as Sawamish County in 1854. Renamed Mason County in 1864, named after Charles Mason, first Secretary and Acting Governor of Washington Territory. Located at the southern end of Puget Sound. The Skokomish Indian Reservation and Squaxin Island Reservation are within the county. Simpson Logging Company dominated the economy.
county_metadata.research_tips: Mason County was originally called Sawamish County (1854-1864), so researchers must check records under both names. The timber industry, particularly Simpson Logging Company (later Simpson Investment Company), dominated the county's economy and employment. Simpson company records can identify workers not found in public records. The Skokomish and Squaxin Island tribes have deep roots in the county — BIA records at NARA Seattle cover both communities. The Shelton-Mason County Historical Society has logging-era photographs and family files. The county's oyster industry on the southern Puget Sound inlets is documented in commercial fishing records. FamilySearch has microfilmed early vital records. County Auditor records date from the 1850s. The Christmas tree industry, centered in Mason County, employed seasonal workers whose records may be found in agricultural labor files.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor recording records from Sawamish County era

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from Sawamish County era. Includes timber company land transactions.

source_profiles.source_name: Skokomish and Squaxin Island Agency Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: BIA records for Skokomish and Squaxin Island tribes. Census rolls, treaty records, allotment files.
Marriage records from territorial era under both county names.
Enumerated as Sawamish County in 1860. Logging camps and mill towns prominent.

source_profiles.source_name: Mason County Superior Court — Probate Records
source_profiles.repository: WA State Archives / Mason County Clerk
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate files including estates of logging company operators and homesteaders.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses, though early records are sparse due to remote terrain.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Stevens County in 1888 during the last territorial year. Named for the Okanogan River. Washington's largest county by area. Borders British Columbia, Canada. The Colville Indian Reservation covers the eastern portion. Mining, ranching, and apple orchards define the economy.
county_metadata.research_tips: Okanogan County is Washington's largest county by area and borders British Columbia, so cross-border family connections are common — check BC vital records and land records for linked families. The Colville Confederated Tribes Reservation covers the eastern portion, and tribal records at NARA Seattle and the Colville tribal offices document families. Before 1888, check records under Stevens County. The gold mining boom at Ruby and Conconully in the 1880s-1890s drew prospectors whose mining claims are at the BLM. The Central Regional Branch of the State Archives in Ellensburg holds Okanogan County records. The Okanogan County Historical Society has ranch-era family files. Apple orchards in the Okanogan and Methow valleys employed seasonal workers. The Methow Valley has attracted homesteaders since the 1890s. FamilySearch has microfilmed early Okanogan County vital records. The county's remote valleys had small communities that are well documented in local newspapers.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Includes mining claims and ranch properties.

Marriage records from county formation.
Federal census from 1890 (destroyed). Shows ranchers, miners, and Native Americans.

county_metadata.parent_county: Original Oregon Territory county (from Lewis County)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Lewis County in 1851 under Oregon Territory, before Washington Territory existed. Named for the Pacific Ocean. One of the oldest counties in Washington. Oysterville was the original county seat until 1893 when the records were famously 'stolen' and moved to South Bend. Willapa Bay oyster industry and Long Beach Peninsula tourism define the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pacific County is notable for one of Washington's most colorful courthouse stories — in 1893, South Bend partisans raided the Oysterville courthouse on a Sunday and physically moved the county records to South Bend, where they remain. This means records are intact but researchers should know records generated before 1893 were created in Oysterville. The Willapa Bay oyster industry drew workers from many backgrounds, including Finnish, Scandinavian, and Chinese oystermen. The county was created in 1851, making its records among Washington's oldest. The Pacific County Historical Society in South Bend has maritime and timber industry collections. Long Beach Peninsula tourism history is documented at the Columbia Pacific Heritage Museum. Finnish immigrants to Naselle and Long Beach left church records in Finnish. FamilySearch has microfilmed early Pacific County records. Shoalwater Bay Reservation (now Shoalwater Bay Indian Tribe) records are at NARA Seattle.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from Oregon Territory era

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from Oregon Territory era. Moved from Oysterville to South Bend in 1893.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good — records survived the county seat move.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from early 1850s. Among the oldest in Washington.
Federal census from 1860. Shows oystermen, fishermen, and timber workers.

source_profiles.source_name: Willapa Bay Oyster Industry Worker Records
source_profiles.repository: Pacific County Historical Society / NARA Seattle
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of Chinese and Japanese oyster workers in Willapa Bay. Chinese Exclusion Act case files.
Early DLCs from Willapa Bay and Columbia River settlements.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Stevens County in 1911, the last county formed in Washington state. Named for the Pend d'Oreille (Kalispel) tribe. Located in the far northeastern corner of Washington bordering Idaho and British Columbia. The Kalispel Indian Reservation is within the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pend Oreille County is the last county formed in Washington (1911) and one of the least populated, making its records compact. Before 1911, check records under Stevens County. Newport, the county seat, sits on the Idaho border and many families had connections to Bonner County, Idaho — check both states. The Kalispel Tribe of Indians has a reservation along the Pend Oreille River, and tribal records at NARA Seattle and the Kalispel tribal offices cover families. The county's logging and mining history is documented at the Pend Oreille County Historical Society in Newport. Box Canyon Dam records at the county PUD document construction workers. The Eastern Regional Branch of the State Archives in Cheney holds Pend Oreille County records. FamilySearch has microfilmed early vital records. Cross-border connections to British Columbia are common — the Metaline Falls border crossing handled regular traffic.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor deed records from formation

Marriage records from county formation. Small volume.
Federal census from 1920 (first after county formation). Pre-1911 enumerated under Stevens County.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM Homestead Patents — Pend Oreille County

county_metadata.parent_county: Original territorial county (from Thurston / Lewis under Oregon Territory)
county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fire losses. Some early Tacoma city records affected by anti-Chinese riots in 1885.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created in 1852 under Oregon Territory. Named after President Franklin Pierce. Tacoma became the county seat and the terminus of the Northern Pacific Railroad in 1873. Joint Base Lewis-McChord (formerly Fort Lewis and McChord Field) is one of the nation's largest military installations. Mount Rainier National Park covers the southeastern corner.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pierce County (Tacoma) was the Northern Pacific Railroad's Pacific terminus, making railroad records essential for research. The Tacoma anti-Chinese riots of 1885 forcibly expelled the Chinese community — records of this event are at the Tacoma Public Library and UW Special Collections. Joint Base Lewis-McChord (Fort Lewis from 1917, McChord Field from 1938) has employed hundreds of thousands of military and civilian personnel — check NARA for service records. The Washington State Historical Society in Tacoma is the state's premier history museum with extensive manuscript and photograph collections. The Tacoma Public Library's Northwest Room has city directories, obituary indexes, and business records. The Puyallup Tribe's reservation is within the county — tribal records at NARA Seattle and the Puyallup Tribal Museum are important for Native American research. FamilySearch has extensive Pierce County microfilm. The Scandinavian community in Tacoma is documented in Nordic heritage collections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Auditor recording division; online index available

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from pre-territorial era. Includes NP Railroad land grants.

source_profiles.source_name: Fort Lewis / JBLM Military Personnel Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Personnel and unit records from one of the largest military bases in the US. Covers both world wars, Korea, and Vietnam-era deployments.
Marriage records from territorial era.
BIA records for the Puyallup Tribe including allotment records, census rolls, and land claims. The Puyallup Reservation borders Tacoma.

source_profiles.source_name: Tacoma Anti-Chinese Records and Immigration Files
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records documenting the 1885 expulsion of Chinese residents and subsequent Chinese Exclusion Act case files.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partial — scattered across Tacoma Public Library Northwest Room and NARA Seattle.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census from 1860. Tacoma shows rapid growth as railroad terminus.

source_profiles.source_name: Tacoma Daily Ledger and Tacoma News Tribune
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Major regional newspapers with extensive coverage of the railroad, military, and timber industries.
